title: The Middle Ages
overview:
The period after the Norman Conquest up until about 1485 is called the Middle Ages (or the medieval period). It was a time of almost constant war, feudalism, the devastating Black Death plague, and important political developments including the Magna Carta and the growth of Parliament.

This lesson covers the wars of the Middle Ages, how Parliament began to develop, the feudal system, the Black Death, the development of the English language and identity, and the Wars of the Roses.

study_sections.heading: War at Home and Abroad
study_sections.content:
The English kings fought with the Welsh, Scottish and Irish noblemen for control of their lands. In Wales, the English were able to establish their rule. In 1284 King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, which annexed Wales to the Crown of England. Huge castles, including Conwy and Caernarvon, were built to maintain this power. By the middle of the 15th century the last Welsh rebellions had been defeated.

In Scotland, the English kings were less successful. In 1314 the Scottish, led by Robert the Bruce, defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn, and Scotland remained unconquered by the English.

At the beginning of the Middle Ages, Ireland was an independent country. The English first went to Ireland as troops to help the Irish king and remained to build their own settlements. By 1200, the English ruled an area of Ireland known as the Pale, around Dublin.

During the Middle Ages, the English kings also fought a number of wars abroad. Many knights took part in the Crusades, in which European Christians fought for control of the Holy Land. English kings also fought a long war with France, called the Hundred Years War (even though it actually lasted 116 years). One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V's vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French. The English left France in the 1450s.

study_sections.heading: The Feudal System and the Black Death
study_sections.content:
The Normans used a system of land ownership known as feudalism. The king gave land to his lords in return for help in war. Landowners had to send certain numbers of men to serve in the army. Some peasants had their own land but most were serfs. They had a small area of their lord's land where they could grow food. In return, they had to work for their lord and could not move away. The same system developed in southern Scotland. In the north of Scotland and Ireland, land was owned by members of the 'clans' (prominent families).

In 1348, a disease, probably a form of plague, came to Britain. This was known as the Black Death. One third of the population of England died and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales. This was one of the worst disasters ever to strike Britain.

Following the Black Death, the smaller population meant there was less need to grow cereal crops. There were labour shortages and peasants began to demand higher wages. New social classes appeared, including owners of large areas of land (later called the gentry), and people left the countryside to live in the towns. In the towns, growing wealth led to the development of a strong middle class.

study_sections.heading: The Magna Carta and Parliament
study_sections.content:
In the Middle Ages, Parliament began to develop into the institution it is today. Its origins can be traced to the king's council of advisers, which included important noblemen and the leaders of the Church.

There were few formal limits to the king's power until 1215. In that year, King John was forced by his noblemen to agree to a number of demands. The result was a charter of rights called the Magna Carta (which means the Great Charter). The Magna Carta established the idea that even the king was subject to the law. It protected the rights of the nobility and restricted the king's power to collect taxes or to make or change laws. In future, the king would need to involve his noblemen in decisions.

In England, parliaments were called for the king to consult his nobles, particularly when the king needed to raise money. The numbers attending Parliament increased and two separate parts, known as Houses, were established. The nobility, great landowners and bishops sat in the House of Lords. Knights and wealthy people from towns and cities were elected to sit in the House of Commons.

A similar Parliament developed in Scotland with three Houses called Estates: the lords, the commons and the clergy.

This was also a time of development in the legal system. The principle that judges are independent of the government began to be established. In England, judges developed 'common law' by a process of precedence (following previous decisions) and tradition. In Scotland, the legal system developed differently and laws were 'codified' (written down).

study_sections.heading: Language and Culture
study_sections.content:
The Middle Ages saw the development of a national culture and identity. After the Norman Conquest, the king and his noblemen had spoken Norman French and the peasants had continued to speak Anglo-Saxon. Gradually these two languages combined to become one English language.

Some words in modern English - for example, 'park' and 'beauty' - are based on Norman French words. Others - for example, 'apple', 'cow' and 'summer' - are based on Anglo-Saxon words. By 1400, in England, official documents were being written in English, and English had become the preferred language of the royal court and Parliament.

In the years leading up to 1400, Geoffrey Chaucer wrote a series of poems in English about a group of people going to Canterbury on a pilgrimage. This collection of poems is called The Canterbury Tales. It was one of the first books to be printed by William Caxton, the first person in England to print books using a printing press.

In Scotland, many people continued to speak Gaelic and the Scots language also developed. A number of poets began to write in the Scots language. One example is John Barbour, who wrote The Bruce about the Battle of Bannockburn.

The Middle Ages also saw a change in the type of buildings in Britain. Castles were built in many places, partly for defence. Great cathedrals were also built, many of which are still used for worship. Several of the cathedrals had windows of stained glass. The glass in York Minster is a famous example.

England was an important trading nation during this period. English wool became a very important export. People came to England from abroad to trade and to work.

study_sections.heading: The Wars of the Roses
study_sections.content:
In 1455, a civil war was begun to decide who should be king of England. It was fought between the supporters of two families: the House of Lancaster and the House of York. This war was called the Wars of the Roses, because the symbol of Lancaster was a red rose and the symbol of York was a white rose.

The war ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485. King Richard III of the House of York was killed in the battle and Henry Tudor, the leader of the House of Lancaster, became King Henry VII. Henry then married King Richard's niece, Elizabeth of York, and united the two families. Henry was the first king of the House of Tudor. The symbol of the House of Tudor was a red rose with a white rose inside it as a sign that the Houses of York and Lancaster were now allies.

key_facts: The Middle Ages refers to the period after the Norman Conquest up until about 1485.
In 1284, King Edward I introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, annexing Wales to the Crown of England.
Huge castles including Conwy and Caernarvon were built in Wales.
In 1314, Robert the Bruce led the Scottish to defeat the English at the Battle of Bannockburn.
Scotland remained unconquered by the English.
By 1200, the English ruled an area of Ireland known as the Pale, around Dublin.
The Hundred Years War was a war between England and France that actually lasted 116 years.
The Battle of Agincourt in 1415 saw Henry V's army defeat the vastly superior French forces.
The feudal system meant the king gave land to lords in return for help in war.
Serfs could not move away and had to work for their lord.
The Black Death arrived in Britain in 1348.
The Black Death killed one third of the population of England.
The Magna Carta was signed in 1215.
King John was forced by his noblemen to sign the Magna Carta.
Magna Carta means 'the Great Charter'.
The Magna Carta established that even the king was subject to the law.
The House of Lords included nobility, great landowners and bishops.
The House of Commons included knights and wealthy people from towns and cities.
The Scottish Parliament had three Houses called Estates: lords, commons and clergy.
By 1400, official documents in England were written in English.
Geoffrey Chaucer wrote The Canterbury Tales.
William Caxton was the first person in England to print books using a printing press.
The glass in York Minster is a famous example of medieval stained glass.
The Wars of the Roses began in 1455.
Lancaster's symbol was a red rose; York's symbol was a white rose.
The Wars of the Roses ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485.
King Richard III was killed at Bosworth Field.
Henry Tudor became King Henry VII and was the first Tudor king.
Henry VII married Elizabeth of York, uniting the two families.
The Tudor symbol was a red rose with a white rose inside it.
memory_hook: Remember Middle Ages key dates: 1215 (Magna Carta), 1284 (Wales annexed), 1314 (Bannockburn), 1348 (Black Death), 1415 (Agincourt), 1455 (Wars of Roses begin), 1485 (Bosworth, Tudors begin). Think 'M-B-B-B-A-W-B': Magna Carta, Bannockburn, Black Death, Battle of Agincourt, Wars of Roses, Bosworth.

questions.prompt: What period is called the Middle Ages?
options: After the Norman Conquest until about 1485
questions.explanation: The Middle Ages refers to the period after the Norman Conquest up until about 1485.

questions.prompt: When did King Edward I annex Wales to England?
questions.explanation: In 1284, the Statute of Rhuddlan annexed Wales to the Crown of England.

questions.prompt: Which castles were built to maintain English power in Wales?
questions.explanation: Huge castles including Conwy and Caernarvon were built in Wales.

questions.prompt: Who led the Scots against the English at Bannockburn?
questions.explanation: In 1314 the Scottish, led by Robert the Bruce, defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn.

questions.prompt: When was the Battle of Bannockburn?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Bannockburn was in 1314.

questions.prompt: What was the Pale in Ireland?
options: The area around Dublin controlled by the English
questions.explanation: By 1200, the English ruled an area of Ireland known as the Pale, around Dublin.

questions.prompt: How long did the Hundred Years War actually last?
questions.explanation: The Hundred Years War actually lasted 116 years, despite its name.

questions.prompt: When was the Battle of Agincourt?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Agincourt was in 1415, where Henry V's army defeated the French.

questions.prompt: What was feudalism?
options: A system where the king gave land to lords in return for help in war
questions.explanation: The Normans used feudalism - the king gave land to lords in return for military service.

questions.prompt: What were serfs?
options: Peasants who had to work for their lord and could not move away
questions.explanation: Serfs had a small area of their lord's land and had to work for their lord, unable to move away.

questions.prompt: When did the Black Death arrive in Britain?
questions.explanation: In 1348, the Black Death - a devastating plague - came to Britain.

questions.prompt: What proportion of England's population died in the Black Death?
questions.explanation: One third of the population of England died in the Black Death.

questions.prompt: When was the Magna Carta signed?
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta was signed in 1215 when King John was forced by his noblemen to agree to it.

questions.prompt: What does Magna Carta mean?
questions.explanation: Magna Carta means 'the Great Charter' in Latin.

questions.prompt: Who forced King John to sign the Magna Carta?
questions.explanation: King John was forced by his noblemen to agree to the demands of the Magna Carta.

questions.prompt: What did the Magna Carta establish?
options: That even the king was subject to the law
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta established the idea that even the king was subject to the law.

questions.prompt: Who sat in the House of Lords?
questions.explanation: The nobility, great landowners and bishops sat in the House of Lords.

questions.prompt: Who sat in the House of Commons?
options: Knights and wealthy people from towns and cities
questions.explanation: Knights and wealthy people from towns and cities were elected to the House of Commons.

questions.prompt: What were the three Houses of the Scottish Parliament called?
questions.explanation: The Scottish Parliament had three Houses called Estates: the lords, the commons and the clergy.

questions.prompt: By what year was English used in official documents?
questions.explanation: By 1400, official documents in England were being written in English.

questions.prompt: Who wrote The Canterbury Tales?
questions.explanation: Geoffrey Chaucer wrote The Canterbury Tales around 1400.

questions.prompt: Who was the first person in England to print books using a printing press?
questions.explanation: William Caxton was the first person in England to print books using a printing press.

questions.prompt: Which cathedral has famous medieval stained glass?
questions.explanation: The glass in York Minster is a famous example of medieval stained glass.

questions.prompt: When did the Wars of the Roses begin?
questions.explanation: In 1455, a civil war began between the House of Lancaster and the House of York.

questions.prompt: What were the symbols of the two sides in the Wars of the Roses?
questions.explanation: Lancaster's symbol was a red rose and York's symbol was a white rose.

questions.prompt: When did the Wars of the Roses end?
questions.explanation: The Wars of the Roses ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485.

questions.prompt: Who became the first Tudor king?
questions.explanation: Henry Tudor became King Henry VII after defeating Richard III at Bosworth Field.

questions.prompt: Who did Henry VII marry to unite Lancaster and York?
questions.explanation: Henry VII married Elizabeth of York, uniting the two rival families.

questions.prompt: What was the Tudor symbol?
options: A red rose with a white rose inside it
questions.explanation: The Tudor symbol was a red rose with a white rose inside it, showing the union of Lancaster and York.
